name: Mag Mell
description: Mag Mell, meaning 'Plain of Delight' or 'Joyful Plain,' is one of the most prominent Otherworlds in Celtic mythology, particularly within Irish tradition. It's often depicted as an idyllic island paradise, a realm of eternal youth, beauty, joy, and abundance, free from sickness, death, and sorrow. Reaching Mag Mell typically requires an invitation or passage granted by the gods or other supernatural beings, often involving a journey across the sea. It is a place where time flows differently, with mortals sometimes returning to their own world after what seemed like a short visit, only to find that centuries have passed. The land is described as perpetually fertile, filled with vibrant colors, sweet music, and delicious food. It is ruled by deities or powerful figures, and often features prominently in heroic tales, where mortals seek adventure, knowledge, or love within its enchanted borders. Mag Mell represents the ultimate ideal of a perfect afterlife or a glimpse into a more harmonious and blissful existence, embodying the Celtic fascination with the supernatural and the cyclical nature of life and death. It serves as a powerful symbol of hope, promise, and the enduring allure of the unknown.
subtitle: The Celtic Island Paradise of Delight

keyMyths.title: Oisín in Tír na nÓg
keyMyths.description: Oisín, a famous warrior, is invited to Tír na nÓg (often considered synonymous with Mag Mell) by Niamh Chinn Óir. He spends what he believes to be three years there, only to return to Ireland and discover that three hundred years have passed.

keyMyths.title: Bran mac Febail's Voyage
keyMyths.description: Bran mac Febail embarks on a voyage to the Otherworld after receiving a branch with apples from a mysterious woman. His journey leads him to various islands, including what is implied to be Mag Mell, where he experiences supernatural wonders.

sources.author: Peter Berresford Ellis
